Category: Infrastructure & Construction: Construction, Engineering, Building Materials, Real Estate Development
Description: Risks with AI-assisted machinery and human interaction.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with construction site operations and general health and safety principles is helpful, though no prior experience with autonomous machinery or advanced technology is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with autonomous and AI-assisted machinery operating on construction sites, and explain how these risks can affect the safety of workers and site personnel. You will also be able to apply practical guidelines to manage human interaction with autonomous equipment, helping you to prioritise safety and reduce the likelihood of accidents in your working environment.

lessons.Description: This lesson introduces the key risks associated with autonomous and AI-assisted machinery operating on construction sites. You will explore how these machines function alongside human workers, and why understanding their behaviour is essential for maintaining a safe working environment. We will look at common hazards that arise when people and autonomous equipment share the same space, including issues with visibility, communication, and unpredictable machine movements. The lesson is designed for anyone working on or around construction sites, regardless of prior technical knowledge, and uses practical examples to make the content easy to follow. By the end, you will have a clearer understanding of how to work more safely in environments where autonomous equipment is present.
lessons.content: Risks with AI-assisted machinery and human interaction.

slides.subtitle: The future of construction is here.
slides.slidecontent:
• Machines now work without operators
• Sites are changing fast
• New risks need new awareness
• Safety rules must keep pace

slides.subtitle: Technology behind self-operating machines
slides.slidecontent:
• Uses sensors, cameras, and software to navigate
• Follows pre-programmed instructions
• Makes decisions without human input
• Examples: excavators, drones, compaction rollers
• Operates continuously during programmed tasks

slides.subtitle: Equipment dangers you need to identify
slides.slidecontent:
• Blind spots hide nearby workers
• Sensors can fail unexpectedly
• Noise masks machine movement
• Crush zones shift constantly
• Warning lights get overlooked easily

slides.subtitle: When people and equipment collide
slides.slidecontent:
• Workers in blind spots remain undetected
• Sudden stops and direction changes catch people off guard
• Reversing and rotating equipment poses highest collision risk
• Mixed zones without clear people-machine separation
• Plant noise drowns out autonomous system warnings

slides.subtitle: Designated areas for safe operations
slides.slidecontent:
• Machine zones reserved exclusively for autonomous equipment
• Human zones kept strictly separate with barriers
• Clear markings, fencing, and signage define boundaries
• Overlapping zones create collision risk
• Always confirm which zone you're entering

slides.subtitle: System-level safety practices
slides.slidecontent:
• Clear exclusion zones around active machines
• High-visibility clothing and check-in procedures
• Designated safe walkways separate from machine areas
• Regular briefings on autonomous equipment locations
• Shared understanding of stop procedures and signals

slides.subtitle: Daily habits to protect yourself
slides.slidecontent:
• Stay visible to machines always
• Never assume equipment has stopped
• Follow designated pedestrian routes only
• Check blind spots before moving
• Report equipment faults immediately
• Maintain constant situational awareness

slides.subtitle: Key principles for daily practice
slides.slidecontent:
• Always assume machines may move without warning
• Never enter operating zones, even briefly
• Report unexpected equipment behaviour immediately
• Know emergency stop locations before work begins
• Review site procedures regularly for updates
• Maintain high-visibility clothing at all times

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on autonomous equipment on construction sites. If you've visited a construction site recently, you may have noticed things look a little different. Machines are starting to operate on their own, without a person sitting in the cab controlling them. That's not science fiction anymore, it's happening right now on sites across the country and around the world. And as this technology moves forward, the construction industry is changing at a remarkable pace. But with that change comes responsibility. When you introduce powerful, self-operating machinery into a busy working environment, new risks appear, risks that many workers and supervisors simply haven't encountered before. That means we all need to build our awareness and understanding. It also means that the safety rules and procedures we've relied on for years need to be reviewed and updated to reflect this new reality. Throughout this course, we'll explore exactly what that means for you and your site.

Let's get clear on what we actually mean when we talk about autonomous construction equipment. These machines carry out tasks without someone physically operating them. Instead of a person in the cab, they rely on sensors, cameras, and software to understand their surroundings and make decisions. They follow pre-programmed instructions, knowing what to do and where to go independently. This technology is already being used in familiar equipment like excavating diggers, surveying drones flying overhead, and large compaction rollers. These are real machines doing real work right now on construction sites around the world. Understanding how they operate is essential because their decision-making process is fundamentally different from human-operated equipment.

Now let's examine the specific hazards you need to watch out for when working alongside autonomous equipment. These machines are impressive, but they come with very real dangers. They have blind spots just like any large vehicle, and a nearby worker can disappear from the machine's detection range without warning. The sensors these machines rely on can fail, sometimes without any obvious sign that something has gone wrong. On busy construction sites, background noise can easily mask the sound of a machine moving towards you. The crush zones around autonomous equipment shift depending on what the machine is doing at any moment. And those warning lights and signals? People stop noticing them after a while, which creates a serious safety gap. Recognising these equipment-specific hazards is your first line of defense.

Let's look at where things go wrong when people and machines work in the same space. Even advanced autonomous equipment has limitations. It may not detect a worker in a blind spot or struggle to sense someone in poor visibility conditions. Because these machines can stop suddenly or change direction without warning, workers nearby can be caught off guard. One of the biggest risks is being too close to equipment that's reversing or rotating, where collision chances are highest. Mixed zones where people and machines share routes without clear separation create additional dangers. Here's something easily overlooked: noise and vibration from nearby plant machinery can drown out the warning signals that autonomous systems use to alert workers. Understanding these interaction patterns is critical for preventing accidents.

When keeping people safe around autonomous equipment, zone separation is one of the most important concepts. Sites are divided into distinct areas: zones where machines operate and zones where people work, kept strictly apart. Physically, you'll see fencing, cones, or painted ground markings that make boundaries obvious. Signage is placed at edges so workers get clear warnings before approaching machine zones. A common misconception is that slow-moving or stationary machines aren't hazardous. They absolutely can be, especially if zone boundaries are crossed unexpectedly. The machine may start moving again or be operating in ways not immediately visible. The golden rule is simple: before stepping onto any part of the site, always confirm which zone you're entering.

When autonomous machines operate on site, keeping people safe requires coordinated site-wide measures. Clear exclusion zones around active equipment must be established and maintained. High-visibility clothing and proper check-in procedures become non-negotiable basics that everyone must follow. Pedestrian walkways need clear separation from machine operating areas to eliminate confusion about where people should and shouldn't be. Regular briefings are crucial because site conditions change quickly, and everyone needs current information about where autonomous equipment is running. The whole team must understand stop procedures and hand signals being used. These systematic approaches create layers of protection that benefit everyone on site.

Let's focus on what you can do daily to protect yourself around autonomous equipment. These machines don't think like human operators - they rely on sensors and cameras, so you must stay visible at all times by wearing high-visibility gear every day, no exceptions. Always stick to designated pedestrian routes planned to keep you away from machine operating areas. Never assume a machine has stopped just because it looks stationary - it could be pausing before its next movement. Before walking near equipment, check blind spots carefully. If you spot anything unusual - a fault, unexpected movement, anything that doesn't seem right - report it immediately. Don't wait. Your awareness and actions genuinely make a difference to site safety.

Let's conclude with essential reminders to carry with you every day on site. Never assume an autonomous machine is stationary or safe to approach - these machines can move without warning, so always treat them as active. Absolutely never step into a machine's operating zone, even briefly. If you notice any unusual equipment behaviour, report it to your supervisor immediately - don't wait or ignore it. Before your shift begins, know exactly where emergency stop points are located on your site. Keep yourself current by regularly reviewing your site's specific procedures for autonomous equipment, because guidance and technology evolve. These habits are simple, but they form the foundation of working safely with autonomous construction equipment.

Who is this for: Construction workers, site supervisors, project managers, safety officers, equipment operators, site foremen, construction engineers, health and safety coordinators
Team or Dept this is for: Construction, Operations, Health and Safety, Engineering, Project Management
